content: just a heads up that I'm now part of two organizations that need support for functional macro expansion so I've offered to help with that on the issue. that's probably what I'm going to be working on next before the netlink bindings.

I will also work on a few bindgen issues after the patch series -- for that one I looked into Clang and how we could fix it upstream and we discussed it at some point in one of the informal meetings, though a fix in bindgen earlier would be nice (I found a way that we could probably remove the dependency on the library that evaluates the expressions altogether, but I didn't write it yet, so it may not work at all or may be too slow)
If you have time in a few days or so we can chat about it (feel free to ping me!)
sounds great! I'm starting with "basic evaluation of Clang API" because I know very little about it.
that way, hopefully I'll be a bit more knowledgeable by the time we talk :)

so far, what I've seen is that there are two cases with function-like macros. there's the definition which bindgen doesn't need to generate anything for and then there's the expansion of the function-like macros which may be used in constant-like macro expansions and those do need to be evaluated. I've played around with the clang API a bit and I'm not seeing a straightforward way to evaluate the expansion of a macro given that the only thing exposed by clang is the macro name, not the expansion. I have some basic ideas for how we could approach this but I think it involves using using a standalone c preprocessor to get the expansions since the C API for Clang does not seem sufficient. any thoughts so far?

@Miguel Ojeda so after looking full at the clang API and the state of bindgen, I think literally almost any solution that's used to improve bindgen will be necessarily hacky given the missing functionality in clang to use a file as a sort of "environment" and evaluate arbitrary tokens according to the context. I discussed this briefly with @Josh Triplett. I'm wondering if it would be better to add this functionality upstream to Clang so that we do it the right way.

I have to spend a bit more time on the series submissions (sorry!), but as a quick answer for the moment: adding the functionality in Clang is of course the best solution long-term and should be done regardless of whether we get a workaround for bindgen.
that's okay! I saw the email go out and figured you'd be busy for a bit. :)

I spent the weekend digging into Clang's C++ API and unfortunately it's a Clang design problem, not just an API problem. because preprocessor statements aren't kept as AST nodes, even with the C++ API, we would need a temporary file. I was hoping this was just an API problem, but it appears to be an architecture problem.

I posted more broadly in the Rust zulip about this bindgen problem. @Miguel Ojeda I'm not sure if you've started any work on the bindgen workaround but there seems to be someone writing a C preprocessor in Rust. while this sounds promising in theory, this has been proposed before in bindgen and the project ended up getting abandoned so I'm not sure if bindgen will be more skeptical this time of someone proposing the same thing. unfortunately there were no excited LLVM contributors dying to add support for this in Clang so we may need to do a little more asking around to get this done right in LLVM. :)
